subject: English Language Arts
topic: Long Way Down - Elevator Floors and What Will Learns
grade_subject_fit: Grade 8 ELA literary analysis. Students have already read the Long Way Down graphic novel and use the book to analyze each elevator encounter, the character's connection to Will, and the lesson or pressure placed on Will.
unit_context: Four 79-minute classes for about 10 students, including students with dyslexia. After one whole-class model, students work mostly independently with page references, short quotes, panel details, oral rehearsal, and reduced-load options.
assignment_purpose: Students create an Elevator Map and theme explanation showing how the floor encounters challenge Will's thinking about The Rules, grief, revenge, memory, and choice.
final_evidence_target: Completed Elevator Map plus short theme explanation using at least three floor encounters as evidence.
student_task_flow: Review The Rules and elevator structure.
Complete the Buck floor as a whole-class model.
Complete floor entries using the graphic novel or novel.
Use a Theme Ladder to connect floor lessons.
Complete final Elevator Map or written theme response.
success_criteria: I can identify the character connected to each floor.
I can use page references, short quotes, or visual details as evidence.
I can explain what each character teaches, warns, or forces Will to question.
I can connect several floor lessons to a larger theme.

supports_scaffolds.label: Dyslexia-friendly evidence
supports_scaffolds.support: Students may use page references, short phrases, visual details, partner reading, oral rehearsal, or dictation rather than copying long quotations.

supports_scaffolds.label: Reduced-load path
supports_scaffolds.support: Students complete Buck plus three additional floors and one theme sentence while keeping the same learning target.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use page references, lesson-word choices, oral rehearsal, and Buck plus three floors.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Complete all floor entries and final theme response.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Compare how the graphic novel visuals shape the lesson of one floor.

checkpoint_release_logic: Release to independent work after the Buck model.
Move to final response after at least three accurate floor lessons are complete.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep teacher-only answer guidance separate from student pages.
Do not require long copied quotes.
Conference prompt: What does this person make Will think about that he was not thinking about before?
likely_misconceptions: Students may summarize plot without explaining the lesson.
Students may treat The Rules as simple instructions instead of a belief system being challenged.
pacing_shape: Day 1 model; Days 2-3 self-directed evidence building; Day 4 synthesis and assessment.

teacher_guide.title: Teacher Guide - Long Way Down Elevator Floors Unit
teacher_guide.big_idea: The elevator structure lets students track how each character encounter pressures Will's beliefs and complicates revenge.
learning_goals: Students use textual or visual evidence.
Students explain what Will learns or questions.
Students synthesize a theme across floors.
materials: Long Way Down graphic novels or novels

timing.activity: Reconnect to The Rules and Will's goal.
Model the sixth floor with Buck.
Students begin one supported floor entry.
Self-directed analysis for Dani and Uncle Mark.
Self-directed analysis for Mikey, Frick, and Shawn.
Theme Ladder and final Elevator Map or response.

teacher_notes: Use Buck as the shared model because he connects Shawn, Will, and the gun.
Accept page references and panel descriptions as evidence.
Use short conferences instead of whole-class reteaching.
teacher_guide.project_prompt: Create an Elevator Map showing how each floor encounter teaches, warns, or pressures Will and how those lessons build a theme.
matching_bank: Frick -> second floor -> fear and consequence
Shawn -> first floor/lobby -> silence and choice

model.title: Teacher model for Buck
model.sample: Buck gets on at the sixth floor. He is connected to Shawn and to the gun. Buck makes Will face the reality and history of the weapon he is carrying.

teacher_guide.assessment_focus: Assess evidence accuracy, lesson explanation, and theme synthesis.

content.subtitle: Track who enters, what they reveal, and what Will has to reconsider.

rows: Explain the lesson from each floor
Connect the pattern to a theme

task_sheet.title: Student Packet - Long Way Down Elevator Floors
instructions: Use your book or graphic novel while you work.
Use page numbers, short quotes, or visual details as evidence.
After the Buck example, work through floors at your own pace.

tasks.label: Start
tasks.prompt: Explain what The Rules expect Will to do.

tasks.label: Model
tasks.prompt: Use the Buck model to see how a complete floor entry works.

tasks.label: Floor work
tasks.prompt: Complete entries for Dani, Uncle Mark, Mikey, Frick, and Shawn. Use point form if needed.

tasks.label: Theme work
tasks.prompt: Choose three floor lessons that connect. Use them to build one theme sentence.

tasks.label: Completion check
tasks.prompt: I used evidence, explained floor lessons, connected a theme, and checked my work with the rubric.

embedded_supports: Evidence can be a page number, short phrase, panel detail, or visual detail.
Frame: This character makes Will think about ___.
Frame: This floor teaches or warns Will that ___.
success_criteria: I completed the required floor entries.
I used evidence.
I explained what each floor teaches or shows Will.
I connected at least three floors to a theme.

floor_organizer.title: Elevator Floor Organizer
floor_organizer.prompt: Complete one row for each elevator-floor encounter. Evidence may be a page number, short quote, or visual detail.
success_criteria: I named the character.
I showed the connection.
I used evidence.
I explained the lesson or warning.

theme_ladder.title: Theme Ladder - From Floor Lessons to Big Message
theme_ladder.prompt: Move from single floor lessons toward a bigger message about grief, revenge, rules, memory, or choice.
success_criteria: I chose connected floor lessons.
I noticed a pattern.
My theme is a full message, not one word.

final_response_sheet.title: Final Response - Elevator Map and Theme Explanation
final_response_sheet.prompt: Use at least three elevator-floor encounters to explain a theme in Long Way Down. Explain what each character teaches, warns, or forces Will to question.
planning_reminders: Use your floor organizer and Theme Ladder.
Use at least three floors as evidence.
Explain the lesson from each floor.
End with a theme message, not just a topic word.
success_criteria: I used at least three floor encounters.
I connected evidence to lessons or warnings.
I explained a theme about grief, revenge, rules, memory, or choice.

rubric_sheet.title: Long Way Down Elevator Map Rubric
rubric_sheet.purpose_line: Assess floor accuracy, evidence, lesson explanation, and theme synthesis.

criteria.name: Floor understanding
criteria.descriptor: The student accurately identifies characters, relationships, and key floor moments.

criteria.name: Evidence use
criteria.descriptor: The student uses page references, short quotes, or visual details that support the explanation.

criteria.name: Lesson and theme
criteria.descriptor: The student explains what Will learns or questions and connects several floors to a larger theme.

success_criteria: My floor details are accurate.
My evidence matches my explanations.
My theme connects more than one floor.

answer_key.title: Teacher Reference - Elevator Floors and Possible Lessons

entries.teacher_note: Buck connects Shawn, Will, and the gun.
Dani brings innocent loss and childhood memory.
Uncle Mark pushes on story, scripts, and choice.
Mikey shows family grief and revenge cycles.
Frick complicates blame through fear and consequence.
Shawn leaves Will with silence, grief, and choice.

scoring_guidance: Accept oral explanation or page references when written output is brief.

exit_ticket.title: Final Self-Assessment Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: What is the strongest part of your Elevator Map or final response? What would you improve with one more class?
success_criteria: I named one strength.
I named one realistic next step.

days.day_label: Day 1: Model the Elevator Floor Pattern
days.focus: Use Buck to learn the floor-entry pattern.

content.subtitle: Model one elevator-floor entry together before independent work.

task_sheet.title: Day 1 Task Sheet - Buck Model
instructions: Complete Buck as a class model.
Start one independent floor entry if time allows.

tasks.label: The Rules
tasks.prompt: What do The Rules expect Will to do?

tasks.label: Buck model
tasks.prompt: Record Buck's connection, evidence, lesson, and effect on Will.

tasks.label: Try one
tasks.prompt: Choose Dani or Uncle Mark and start one floor entry.

embedded_supports: Evidence can be a page number, short phrase, or visual detail.
success_criteria: I understand the floor-entry pattern.
I helped complete the Buck model.

exit_ticket.title: Day 1 Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: What is the difference between summarizing a floor and explaining the lesson from a floor?
success_criteria: I named the difference.
I used one example.

content.subtitle: Use evidence to explain memory, story, and pressure on Will.

rows: Explain the lesson and effect on Will

task_sheet.title: Day 2 Task Sheet - Dani and Uncle Mark
instructions: Conference after two completed floor entries.

tasks.label: Dani
tasks.prompt: Complete one organizer row for Dani: connection, evidence, lesson, and effect on Will.

tasks.label: Uncle Mark
tasks.prompt: Complete one organizer row for Uncle Mark: connection, evidence, lesson, and effect on Will.

tasks.label: Pattern check
tasks.prompt: What do these two floors have in common? What is different?

embedded_supports: Frame: This character makes Will think about ___.
success_criteria: I completed two floor rows.
I used evidence for both floors.

exit_ticket.title: Day 2 Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: Which floor from today most challenges Will's thinking? Give one piece of evidence and explain why.
success_criteria: I chose one floor.
I used evidence.

days.focus: Finish floor evidence and start pattern finding.

slides.title: Day 3: More Than One Kind of Lesson

content.subtitle: A floor can teach through regret, fear, silence, memory, or consequence.

task_sheet.title: Day 3 Task Sheet - Mikey, Frick, and Shawn
instructions: Start the Theme Ladder when you have at least three completed floors.

tasks.label: Mikey
tasks.prompt: Complete one organizer row for Mikey. Focus on family, grief, and cycles.

tasks.label: Frick
tasks.prompt: Complete one organizer row for Frick. Focus on fear, mistake, and consequence.

tasks.label: Shawn
tasks.prompt: Complete one organizer row for Shawn. Focus on brotherhood, silence, grief, and choice.

tasks.label: Theme start
tasks.prompt: Choose three floor lessons that seem connected. What pattern do you notice?

embedded_supports: Lesson words: grief, fear, mistake, memory, revenge, choice, pressure, regret, family, silence.
success_criteria: I completed enough floors to build a theme.
I started connecting floors into a pattern.

exit_ticket.title: Day 3 Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: Write one possible theme sentence for Long Way Down. It should be a message, not one word.
success_criteria: My theme is a full message.
My theme connects to more than one floor.

days.day_label: Day 4: Final Elevator Map and Theme Explanation

content.subtitle: Use floor evidence to explain a larger theme.

rows: Explain the lesson from each floor
Connect the pattern to a theme

task_sheet.title: Day 4 Task Sheet - Final Completion Check
instructions: Choose the full or reduced-load final path.
Use your floor organizer and Theme Ladder.
Check your work with the rubric before handing it in.

tasks.label: Final path
tasks.prompt: Circle your final path: full Elevator Map / reduced-load Elevator Map / written theme response.

tasks.label: Evidence check
tasks.prompt: List the three floors you used as evidence.

tasks.label: Theme check
tasks.prompt: Write your theme sentence. It should be a message, not just a topic word.

embedded_supports: Theme frame: The novel suggests that ___.
Evidence frame: One floor that shows this is ___.
success_criteria: I used at least three floors.
I connected evidence to a theme.

exit_ticket.title: Day 4 Exit Ticket
exit_ticket.prompt: What is the strongest part of your Elevator Map or final response? What would you improve with one more class?
success_criteria: I named one strength.
I named one realistic next step.
